case_name: Cobalt removal from well water for small drinking water supply with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for cobalt-contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply under a minimal-chemical-use operating preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small community well has elevated dissolved cobalt in otherwise relatively clean groundwater and needs a practical treatment upgrade for drinking water production. The operator prefers established technologies that avoid heavy routine chemical dosing and can be run with low to moderate operational attention. The utility can manage periodic media replacement or backwashing but wants to avoid complex sludge-generating treatment trains unless clearly justified.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable cobalt reduction for drinking water supply while maintaining stable finished water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven and practical small-system treatment
keep energy demand moderate or low

core_unit_functions.adsorptive_media: remove dissolved cobalt by sorption onto selective metal-oxide or mineral-based media
core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_pressure_filtration: retain media fines and provide final particulate polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: the raw well water has low turbidity and low DOC, which is favorable for fixed-bed adsorption performance
cobalt is the clear primary contaminant, so a targeted polishing process is appropriate
adsorptive media can achieve removal with little or no continuous chemical dosing
this approach aligns well with small-system preference for simple, robust operation and limited residuals
potential_risks: media exhaustion requires monitoring and timely replacement
actual cobalt capacity can be reduced by competing ions or site-specific water chemistry
pilot or bench verification may be needed to confirm breakthrough behavior

core_unit_functions.cation_exchange: remove dissolved cobalt ions through exchange on resin sites
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish effluent and capture any resin carryover or particulates
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection in finished water

applicability_rationale: cobalt is present as a dissolved divalent metal and can be treated by cation exchange under suitable chemistry
the source water is relatively clean and low in suspended solids, which supports resin operation
chemical use during routine service is limited compared with precipitation-based treatment
the process is compact and technically established for dissolved metal removal applications
potential_risks: hardness ions such as calcium and magnesium can compete strongly and shorten run length
regeneration brine handling can be a management issue
resin fouling or selectivity limitations may reduce practical performance

core_unit_functions.oxidation: promote conversion of dissolved cobalt to forms more amenable to capture or surface precipitation
core_unit_functions.catalytic_media_filtration: retain oxidized or surface-bound metal species on filter media

applicability_rationale: a light oxidation step followed by filtration can be feasible for some dissolved metals in groundwater
the process can remain simpler than full coagulation-clarification trains
backwashable media filtration is familiar to many small utilities
potential_risks: cobalt oxidation may be incomplete at near-neutral pH without optimized oxidant choice and contact time
removal may be less predictable than adsorption for trace dissolved cobalt
process performance can depend strongly on influent speciation and filter surface chemistry

core_unit_functions.chemical_precipitation: convert dissolved cobalt into insoluble hydroxide or related solids
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: aggregate fine precipitates into removable flocs

applicability_rationale: precipitation-based metal removal is a conventional and well-understood treatment concept
it can achieve substantial cobalt removal when pH and solids separation are controlled
it offers a fallback option if adsorption or ion exchange are unsuitable at full scale
potential_risks: requires more routine chemical dosing than preferred in this case
produces sludge requiring handling and disposal
careful pH control is needed and post-stabilization may be necessary

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved cobalt and other ions through high-pressure membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and distribution stability

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis is technically capable of reducing dissolved cobalt
it may be considered if future multi-contaminant control becomes necessary
potential_risks: high energy demand compared with targeted adsorption or exchange
concentrate management burden is significant for a small well system
this is more complex than needed for a single-contaminant case with minimal chemical use preference

preferred_rank_pattern: targeted low-chemical-use adsorption should rank first for relatively clean well water with single dissolved cobalt contamination
ion_exchange may rank near the top but below adsorption when hardness and brine management are relevant
high-chemical or high-energy options should not rank first under the stated constraints

must_reflect: minimal routine chemical addition is a central selection factor
small-system practical operation favors simple fixed-bed or compact treatment
sludge-intensive treatment should be penalized relative to media-based options
membrane treatment should rank lower because the case does not justify high complexity

must_contain: why adsorptive media is attractive for low-turbidity well water and single-metal treatment
why ion exchange is feasible but affected by competing cations and regeneration needs
why precipitation-based treatment conflicts with minimal chemical use
why reverse osmosis is technically effective but not preferred here

should_mention: competition from hardness ions or other dissolved species
brine or spent media disposal requirements
uncertainty in cobalt speciation and treatability without site testing
